title: Social proof must include verifiable details
summary: AI-generated marketing sections include social proof without verifiable details, making it feel manufactured.
instruction: Every social proof element must include identifying details: company name for logos, full name for testimonials, methodology for metrics.
rationale: Social proof only works if users believe it. Vague testimonials ('Amazing product - John'), unlabeled logos, and unverifiable metrics trigger skepticism rather than trust.

violations: Testimonials attributed to first names only with blurry avatars.
A logo grid of unrecognizable logos with no labels.
controls: Testimonials with full name, title, company, photo, referencing specific outcomes.
Logo grid with recognizable names and optional permission statements.
borderline: Startups with early customers may not have recognizable logos - names and context suffice.

exceptions.description: Enterprise sales pages where customer logos are under NDA. Internal tool landing pages where the audience knows the customer base.

detector.signalDescription: Review testimonial elements, logo lists, and metric counters for identifiable details.
limitations: Requires semantic understanding of what constitutes verifiable details for each context.

scenarios: Recognizable Fortune 500 logos needing no text labels.
Startups with legitimate but unknown early customers.
mitigations: Logos of well-known brands need no text labels. Flag only when logos are obscure AND unlabeled.

fixturePlan.description: Testimonials with first names only, generic stock photos, unlabeled logos.
Testimonials with full details, specific outcomes, recognized logos.
Startup page with real customer names and companies even if not well-known.

notes: Normalization note: status changed from review-ready to draft. Manual-only feasibility is correctly assigned. This is a design-review checklist item, not a current linter rule. Verifying that social proof elements include identifiable details requires understanding of brand recognition, industry context, and customer permission boundaries that automation cannot reliably assess. The rule is useful as editorial guidance but should not be implemented as a mechanical detector.
